title: Fine-Tuning & PEFT — LoRA, QLoRA, DoRA, Adapters
description: At Staff level, choose the right adaptation strategy: LoRA for task adaptation (~0.1% params), full FT for domain adaptation, multi-task LoRA fusion for serving 50+ customers on one base model. Understand when PEFT is sufficient and when only full fine-tuning will close the quality gap.

sections.title: Full Fine-Tuning vs PEFT
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters on the target dataset. It can change the model behavior dramatically — a model can unlearn pre-training knowledge (catastrophic forgetting). PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning) methods like LoRA update a tiny fraction of parameters while keeping the base model frozen. The base model's knowledge is preserved, and the adapter only learns the 'delta' for the target task.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Full FT of Llama 3 7B: 7B parameters × 2 bytes (BF16) = 14GB optimizer states (Adam: m + v = 28GB), 14GB gradients, 14GB weights = ~70GB total. Needs at least 2 A100-80GB (or FSDP). LoRA (r=8 on all attention projections): ~7M trainable params (0.1% of 7B), needs ~16GB GPU memory (12GB for frozen base model + 4GB for LoRA weights + optimizer states). A single RTX 4090 (24GB) can fine-tune a 7B with LoRA.

track_changes Decision Rule: Full FT for domain adaptation (the domain's language/distribution differs substantially from pre-training: medical, legal, code). LoRA for task adaptation (same domain, new task: classification, extraction, summarization). If you're unsure, start with LoRA — if it plateaus too low (<5% gap to target), escalate to full FT.
sections.codeBlock:
# Full fine-tuning vs LoRA — memory and param comparison
import math

def ft_memory(params_b, batch_size=1, seq_len=4096, dtype_bytes=2):
    """Estimate full fine-tuning GPU memory."""
    n_params = params_b * 1e9
    weights = n_params * dtype_bytes
    grads = n_params * dtype_bytes  # Same size as weights
    optimizer = n_params * 4 * dtype_bytes  # Adam: m, v states (4 bytes each in mixed)
    activations = batch_size * seq_len * n_params * dtype_bytes / (1024**3)
    total_gb = (weights + grads + optimizer + activations) / 1e9
    return total_gb

def lora_memory(params_b, r=8, n_modules=16, dtype_bytes=2):
    """Estimate LoRA training memory (base model frozen + adapters)."""
    n_params = params_b * 1e9
    base_weights = n_params * dtype_bytes  # Frozen, no grads needed
    # LoRA: A ∈ R^{d×r}, B ∈ R^{r×k}, for each target module
    d_model = 4096  # Llama 3 7B default
    lora_params = n_modules * 2 * d_model * r  # A + B per module
    lora_weights = lora_params * dtype_bytes
    lora_grads = lora_params * dtype_bytes
    lora_optimizer = lora_params * 4 * dtype_bytes  # Adam for LoRA params
    total_gb = (base_weights + lora_weights + lora_grads + lora_optimizer) / 1e9
    return total_gb

print(f"Full FT  7B: {ft_memory(7):.0f} GB")
print(f"LoRA FT  7B: {lora_memory(7):.0f} GB")
print(f"Full FT 70B: {ft_memory(70):.0f} GB (needs 3× A100-80GB)")
print(f"LoRA FT 70B: {lora_memory(70):.0f} GB (fits on 1× A100")

sections.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LoRA hypothesizes that weight updates during fine-tuning have low intrinsic rank. Instead of updating the full weight matrix W ∈ ℝ^{d×k}, LoRA learns a low-rank decomposition: W' = W + BA, where B ∈ ℝ^{d×r}, A ∈ ℝ^{r×k}, and r ≪ min(d, k). The frozen W retains pre-trained knowledge, while BA learns the task-specific delta.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: For Llama 3 7B (d_model=4096), r=8 means each LoRA adapter has 4096×8 + 8×4096 = 65,536 params per module. Targeting all attention Q, K, V, O projections (16 modules in 32 layers × 4 = 128 modules) = 128 × 65,536 = ~8.4M trainable params out of 7B = 0.12%. r=8 is the 'goldilocks' rank for most tasks. r=64 (0.96% params) for harder domain adaptation. r > 128 shows diminishing returns — full FT would be better at that point.

search Interview Angle: "Why initialize A as random and B as zeros?" Two reasons: 1) At initialization, BA = 0 (since B = 0), so W' = W — the model starts from the pre-trained checkpoint with no behavior change. Training smoothly introduces the delta. 2) If both were random uniform, W' would be a noisy version of W at step 0, and the optimizer would waste steps undoing the noise. Zero initialization ensures the model starts exactly at the pre-trained weights.
sections.codeBlock:
# LoRA implementation — low-rank adapter for nn.Linear
import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F

class LoRALayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, out_dim, r=8, alpha=16):
        super().__init__()
        self.r = r
        self.scaling = alpha / r  # alpha scales the update magnitude
        # A: random normal (small variance so output is small at init)
        self.A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(in_dim, r) * 0.02)
        # B: zeros so BA = 0 at initialization
        self.B = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(r, out_dim))

    def forward(self, x, base_weight):
        # LoRA output = Wx + s * (x @ A @ B)
        return F.linear(x, base_weight) + self.scaling * (x @ self.A @ self.B)

# Merge LoRA into base weights for inference (zero overhead)
def merge_lora(base_weight: torch.Tensor, lora: LoRALayer) -> torch.Tensor:
    """W' = W + α/r * BA (merged for inference, no extra compute)."""
    return base_weight + lora.scaling * (lora.A @ lora.B)

# Usage during training — only backprop through A and B
# Example: Llama 3 7B, target nn.Linear(4096, 4096) for Q projection
# q_proj = LoRALayer(4096, 4096, r=8)
# optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(q_proj.parameters(), lr=2e-4)
# ponytail: r=8 for task, r=64 for domain, r=256 ≈ full FT at 2× cost

sections.title: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: QLoRA (Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation) pushes LoRA further by quantizing the frozen base model to 4-bit while keeping the LoRA adapters in full precision. This reduces the memory footprint of the base model by 4× (FP16 → NF4), making it feasible to fine-tune models like Llama 3 70B on a single A100 (80GB).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: NF4 (NormalFloat4) is a 4-bit quantization data type specifically designed for normally distributed weights. It allocates quantization levels optimally for a Gaussian distribution, resulting in less quantization error than standard uniform 4-bit for LLM weights. QLoRA matches full FT performance within 1-2% on common benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K) while using 4× less memory for the base model. Llama 3 70B QLoRA: base model in NF4 = 35GB, LoRA params (r=16) = ~0.5GB, optimizer states = ~2GB, activations = ~2GB → total ~40GB, comfortably fitting on a single A100-80GB.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Load base model in NF4 (bitsandbytes, transformers). 2) Attach LoRA adapters in BF16. 3) Train with paged AdamW (offload optimizer states to CPU if needed). 4) Save only LoRA weights (~16MB for 7B, ~50MB for 70B). 5) At inference, merge LoRA into the FP16 model or keep NF4 + LoRA (no merge needed).
sections.codeBlock:
# QLoRA: 4-bit NF4 quantization with LoRA
import torch, bitsandbytes as bnb

class NF4Quantizer:
    """Simulate NF4 (NormalFloat4) quantization for weight storage."""
    # NF4 codebook: 16 levels optimized for N(0, 1)
    CODEBOOK = torch.tensor([-1.0, -0.6962, -0.5254, -0.3944, -0.2798, -0.1704,
                              -0.0586,  0.0584,  0.1762,  0.2996,  0.4326,  0.5838,
                               0.7654,  1.0,     1.3334,  2.0])

    @staticmethod
    def quantize(weights: torch.Tensor):
        """Quantize weights to NF4 (2 bytes per 2 elements)."""
        absmax = weights.abs().max()
        scaled = weights / (absmax / 3.0)  # Scale to [-3, 3]
        # Quantize to nearest codebook entry
        indices = torch.bucketize(scaled, NF4Quantizer.CODEBOOK) - 1
        indices = indices.clamp(0, 15)
        return indices.to(torch.uint8), absmax

    @staticmethod
    def dequantize(indices: torch.Tensor, absmax: float):
        """Dequantize on-the-fly during forward pass."""
        return NF4Quantizer.CODEBOOK[indices.long()] * (absmax / 3.0)

# QLoRA training loop (conceptual)
# from transformers import BitsAndBytesConfig, AutoModelForCausalLM
#
# bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
#     load_in_4bit=True,
#     bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
#     bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
#     bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
# )
# model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
#     "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B",
#     quantization_config=bnb_config,
#     device_map="auto",
# )
# # LoRA adapters in BF16 (base model stays in NF4)
# lora_config = LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32, target_modules=["q_proj","v_proj"])
# model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
# # Trainer.train() — only LoRA params get gradients

sections.title: DoRA: Weight-Decomposed LoRA
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: DoRA (Decomposed Rank Adaptation) stems from the observation that full fine-tuning changes both the magnitude (norm) and direction (angular alignment) of weight vectors. LoRA only adapts direction implicitly. DoRA explicitly decomposes the weight update: W' = m * (W + BA) / ||W + BA||, where m is a learnable magnitude vector. This lets the model independently control 'how important' (magnitude) vs 'what pattern' (direction).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: DoRA outperforms LoRA by 2-5% on reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH, ARC-Challenge) at the same rank. The magnitude decomposition adds only ~0.01% extra parameters (d_model=4096 → 4096 m parameters) per layer — negligible. At inference, DoRA merges into a single weight matrix with zero inference overhead (no extra operations).

search Staff Engineer Question: "When would you choose DoRA over LoRA?" 1) For reasoning-heavy tasks where the 2-5% accuracy improvement matters. 2) When fine-tuning on small datasets (<1K examples) where LoRA tends to overfit — DoRA's magnitude regularization helps. 3) For multi-task adaptation where each task may need different activation magnitudes (the magnitude vector acts as a per-output-channel task selector). Use plain LoRA for simple classification or extraction tasks where the extra complexity doesn't buy meaningful gains.
sections.codeBlock:
# DoRA: Weight-decomposed low-rank adaptation
import torch, torch.nn as nn

class DoRALayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, out_dim, r=8, alpha=16):
        super().__init__()
        # LoRA-like low-rank update (direction)
        self.A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(in_dim, r) * 0.02)
        self.B = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(r, out_dim))
        self.scaling = alpha / r
        # Learnable magnitude vector (one per output channel)
        self.m = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(out_dim))

    def forward(self, x, base_weight):
        # W' = (BA) — low-rank update in direction space
        lora_update = self.A @ self.B
        # W_merged = base_weight + α/r * lora_update (direction)
        W_dir = base_weight + self.scaling * lora_update
        # Normalize by column norm, then scale by m
        # W' = m * W_dir / ||W_dir||_c
        W_dir_normed = W_dir / (W_dir.norm(dim=0, keepdim=True) + 1e-8)
        return F.linear(x, W_dir_normed * self.m.unsqueeze(0))

    def merge(self, base_weight):
        """Merge into single weight for inference (zero overhead)."""
        W_dir = base_weight + self.scaling * (self.A @ self.B)
        return W_dir / (W_dir.norm(dim=0, keepdim=True) + 1e-8) * self.m.unsqueeze(0)

# DoRA outperforms LoRA by 2-5% on reasoning at same rank
# Key insight: magnitude (m) controls output scale independently of
# direction (BA). Full FT often changes magnitudes significantly;
# LoRA without magnitude adaptation under-corrects for this.

sections.title: Multi-Task Fine-Tuning & Adapter Fusion
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Serving 50+ customers on a single base model without 50 model copies: train one LoRA adapter per task/customer, then fuse the relevant adapters during inference based on the incoming request. This is the production pattern for SaaS LLM platforms. Each adapter is tiny (~16MB for 7B), and the base model is shared.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: 10 tasks, shared base model: <1% memory per task (each LoRA adapter ~50MB for 70B, r=8). Adapter fusion (weighted average of adapter weights) achieves 90% of full multi-task quality at 1% of the cost. Full multi-task FT requires a large dataset mixing all 10 tasks and often suffers from task interference (catastrophic forgetting between tasks). Adapter fusion sidesteps this entirely.

track_changes Production Architecture: 1) Train individual LoRA adapters per task (separate dataset, separate training run). 2) Store adapters in a model registry (keyed by task_id). 3) During inference: load base model once, load the requested adapter(s), merge them. 4) For multi-task queries: fuse adapters via weighted average: W_merged = W_base + Σ(w_i × BA_i). 5) Monitor per-task adapter drift (re-train if task distribution shifts).

search Interview Angle: "How would you fine-tune a model for 50 different customers without 50 model copies?" Use LoRA adapters with a shared base model, routed by customer ID. Each adapter is stored as a separate checkpoint (~16MB for 7B). At inference, load the base model once into GPU memory, load the per-request adapter on-the-fly from CPU/SSD (50MB at 70B scale, ~1ms to transfer over PCIe). Hot adapters can be cached in a LRU cache on GPU (e.g., top 10 most active customers' adapters always loaded). For a customer with a combination of needs, fuse their adapter with a general-purpose adapter using learned routing weights.

sections.title: Domain Adaptation: Medical, Legal, Code
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Domain adaptation shifts the model from general-purpose to domain-specialist. Unlike task adaptation (which teaches new behavior), domain adaptation teaches the model the language, style, and knowledge of a specific field. This typically requires continued pre-training on a large domain corpus before any task-specific fine-tuning.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Domain continued pre-training on 500B tokens of medical text improves medical benchmarks (MedQA, PubMedQA) by 5-15%. CodeLlama started from Llama 2 and continued pre-training on 500B tokens of code → HumanEval pass@1 from 30% (Llama 2 34B) to 67% (CodeLlama 34B). A study on legal domain adaptation: continued pre-training on 150B legal tokens improved bar exam performance by 8% over base Llama 2. Key finding: domain continued pre-training is 10-100× more sample-efficient than domain-specific supervised fine-tuning for knowledge acquisition.

track_changes Domain Adaptation Workflow: 1) Phase 0 – Data audit: collect 50B-500B domain tokens; deduplicate, filter quality, ensure legal compliance. 2) Phase 1 – Continued pre-training: train with causal LM loss, small learning rate (1/10 of pre-training LR), for 10-20% of original pre-training steps. 3) Phase 2 – Task adaptation: LoRA on domain-specific supervised datasets (question-answering, summarization, classification). 4) Phase 3 – RLHF/DPO: align to domain-specific preferences (e.g., citation format for legal, code safety for programming).

track_changes Cost-Escalation Rule: Start with LoRA on the domain dataset (Phase 2 only, cheapest). If quality plateaus, add Phase 1 continued pre-training (medium cost). Only escalate to full FT (expensive) if Phases 1+2 together are still insufficient. Most domain adaptations are solved by Phase 2 (LoRA) + Phase 3 (DPO).
sections.codeBlock:
# Domain continued pre-training loop
import torch, torch.nn as nn

def domain_continued_pretrain(model, dataloader, tokenizer, steps=10000):
    """Phase 1: continued pre-training on domain corpus."""
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-5)  # 10× smaller than pre-training
    scheduler = torch.optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=steps)
    model.train()

    for step, batch in enumerate(dataloader):
        if step >= steps:
            break
        input_ids = batch["input_ids"]
        labels = input_ids.clone()  # Causal LM: predict next token

        outputs = model(input_ids=input_ids, labels=labels)
        loss = outputs.loss
        loss.backward()
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
        optimizer.step()
        scheduler.step()
        optimizer.zero_grad()

        if step % 1000 == 0:
            print(f"Step {step}: loss = {loss.item():.4f}")

    return model

# ponytail: 500B tokens at 8K tokens/batch, 10K steps = 80M tokens seen
# This is ~0.016% of total training — sufficient for domain adaptation
# because the model already knows language structure; it's learning
# domain-specific token co-occurrence patterns

sections.title: Fine-Tuning Data Curation
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The quality of your fine-tuning data matters more than its quantity. The principle: "Cover the distribution you serve, not the one you pre-trained on." A pre-trained model already knows general language; fine-tuning data should teach the input-output mapping for your specific task. 1K carefully curated examples beat 50K noisy web-scraped pairs.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: In controlled experiments: 1K high-quality instruction pairs achieve the same Llama 3 8B downstream performance as 10K medium-quality pairs (LIMA paper, 2023). Diversity matters more than volume: 2K diverse examples (covering all subcategories of the task) outperform 10K homogeneous examples by 5-10% on held-out test sets. Deduplication: removing near-duplicate examples (cosine similarity > 0.85) typically removes 10-30% of fine-tuning datasets without quality loss.

track_changes Data Curation Checklist:

  1. Coverage: annotate each example with task subcategory; ensure all subcategories have ≥10 examples.
  2. Correctness: manually audit a random 5% of examples — flag any with wrong answers for re-labeling.
  3. Consistency: ensure formatting is uniform (e.g., all answers in JSON, consistent CoT format).
  4. Deduplication: use embedding similarity to remove near-duplicates (threshold 0.85).
  5. Balance: check label distribution — oversample rare classes to at least 5% of the dataset.
  6. Edge cases: include adversarial examples (ambiguous inputs, missing information, contradictory instructions).

search Staff Engineer Question: "Your team scraped 1M instruction pairs from web data. Fine-tuning on all of it degrades performance. What do you do?" 1) Audit a sample: my guess is >50% have incorrect or low-quality answers (web data is noisy). 2) Use a stronger LLM (GPT-4, Claude 3.5) to score quality on a 1-5 scale; filter to top-20%. 3) Deduplicate. 4) Balance across task categories. 5) The result will be ~50K high-quality examples. 6) Fine-tune on this filtered set and evaluate against a held-out golden test set. 7) If still degraded, reduce to the top 10K examples. Less is often more for fine-tuning.
sections.codeBlock:
# Fine-tuning data curation pipeline
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity

def filter_dataset(examples, quality_scores, quality_threshold=3.0, diversity_threshold=0.85):
    """Filter by quality score, then deduplicate by embedding similarity."""
    # Phase 1: Quality filter
    filtered = [ex for ex, score in zip(examples, quality_scores)
                if score >= quality_threshold]
    print(f"Quality filter: {len(examples)} → {len(filtered)}")

    # Phase 2: Deduplication
    embeddings = np.array([ex["embedding"] for ex in filtered])
    keep_mask = np.ones(len(filtered), dtype=bool)

    for i in range(len(filtered)):
        if not keep_mask[i]:
            continue
        for j in range(i + 1, len(filtered)):
            if not keep_mask[j]:
                continue
            sim = cosine_similarity(embeddings[i:i+1], embeddings[j:j+1])[0, 0]
            if sim > diversity_threshold:
                keep_mask[j] = False  # Remove duplicate (j later)

    deduped = [f for f, k in zip(filtered, keep_mask) if k]
    print(f"Deduplication: {len(filtered)} → {len(deduped)}")
    return deduped

# Staff insight: 1K high-quality > 50K noisy
# Measure: fine-tune on 1K, 10K, 50K subsets, eval on golden set
# The Pareto curve often saturates at ~2-5K examples for task FT

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (Hu et al., 2021) — The original LoRA paper. Shows that weight updates in LLMs have low intrinsic rank and can be decomposed into low-rank matrices.
  • QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized Language Models (Dettmers et al., 2023) — NF4 quantization + LoRA. Enables 70B fine-tuning on a single A100 with less than 1% quality loss.
  • DoRA: Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation (Liu et al., 2024) — Decomposes updates into magnitude + direction. Outperforms LoRA by 2-5% at same rank with zero inference overhead.
  • LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment (Zhou et al., 2023) — Shows that 1K high-quality examples can match 50K noisy ones for fine-tuning. The foundational 'data quality over quantity' paper.
  • Scaling Data-Constrained Language Models (Muennighoff et al., 2023) — Data curation and scaling laws under constrained data regimes. Relevant for domain adaptation with limited domain text.

details: PEFT Decision Tree: Task adaptation → LoRA (r=8, 0.1% params). Domain adaptation → QLoRA (NF4 base + LoRA r=64). Reasoning-heavy → DoRA (>LoRA 2-5%). Multi-task → Adapter fusion (shared base + per-task LoRA). Data-starved (<500 examples) → DoRA with magnitude regularization. If LoRA plateaus >5% below target → full FT.
